data.title: Republicans are sinking, but Democrats aren't rising
data.description:
The clearest signal yet about the 2026 midterms may not be coming from national polling, but from Virginia.

In a significant rebuke to Republican redistricting efforts, Old Dominion voters just passed a new map that should flip four House seats to Democrats, undercutting what had been viewed as a key structural advantage for Republicans heading into November.

As we have described in the past, that caused the nonpartisan forecaster Sabato’s Crystal Ball to predict 217 seats leaning Democrat, 205 leaning Republican and 13 tossups.  

This is the latest example of Trump’s midterm gambit backfiring and an early sign that even aggressive attempts to shape the electoral playing field may not be enough to overcome the political headwinds facing Republicans. 

At the same time, those headwinds are becoming increasingly evident to Republicans themselves. Indeed, Senate Republicans may have just tipped their hand about their confidence – or lack thereof. In recent days, several Republican senators have urged President Trump to make any pending Cabinet shakeups immediately, not-so-subtly pointing to growing uncertainty about their narrow Senate majority. 

And the Republicans’ concern is well-founded. According to RealClearPolitics, Trump’s 41 percent approval rating — 17.5 points underwater — is at record lows. Democrats lead the generic congressional ballot by 6 points. Meanwhile, only 34 percent of Americans feel the country is heading in the “right direction.”

Moreover, on the economy, often the decisive issue, a remarkable 47 percent of Americans describe the economy as “poor,” while Gallup’s Economic Confidence Index has fallen to minus-38, the lowest reading since November 2023.  

Typically, partisan attitudes color these surveys, with voters more optimistic if their party is in control. Yet even partisan loyalty is not helping the Republicans. A recent Fox News poll found that a majority of Republicans rate the economy (56 percent) and their personal financial situation (52 percent) negatively. 

The Virginia result adds to this broader pattern and also aligns with another trend we have previously noted: Democrats appear more enthusiastic. By a considerable 17-point margin, Democrats are more likely than Republicans to say they’re “extremely motivated” to vote per CNN — a potential turnout edge that could prove decisive in a close election.

Another key factor hurting Republicans but sparing Democrats is that the former must also govern while they campaign.

A recent article in the New York Times exposed that some Republicans “are frustrated” that they’re spending valuable time on a reconciliation bill funding the Department of Homeland Security and other legislation that may be important for governing, but which voters tend to ignore. This has led Republicans to “worry they might not get another chance before they face voters this fall to push through legislation with real political appeal.” 

Despite these advantages, Democrats are far from secure. Polling consistently suggests that Democrats’ advantage is largely due to frustration with Trump, rather than a belief that Democrats’ policies would be better. The Fox News survey shows that voters only give Democrats a 4-point advantage on the economy, a virtual tie given the 3-point margin of error. On border security, crime, immigration and national security, voters continue to prefer Republicans.

Further, Democrats continue to struggle with negative perceptions of their party. The CNN poll also revealed that just 28 percent of Americans rate Democrats favorably, lower than Republicans’ (also poor) 32 percent.

It also pointed out that, compared to the midterms in Trump’s first term, Democrats’ net favorability has declined “by nearly 30 points” since 2018. Internal divisions compound the problem. Roughly two-thirds of Democratic voters say the party is facing major disagreements over priorities and ideological direction. 

In that vein, there has recently been considerable attention paid to Hasan Piker, the controversial influencer whom progressives have venerated yet whose extremism and anti-American tirades worry moderates — and give Republicans ammunition.

Piker has campaigned with or interviewed multiple Democrats, including Reps. Ro Khanna (D-Calif.) and Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez (D-N.Y.). But the issue is bigger than any one person. Piker’s prominence is just one example of the party being pulled away from the more moderate positioning that most voters still prefer. This trend may alienate the very swing voters Democrats need to win.  

As Juan Williams has written, the party’s embrace of Piker’s extremism risks “splitting Democrats, opening the door to Republican wins in the midterms.” Williams continued, “This is not just about one podcaster. It’s about the future of the Democratic Party.” 

Ultimately, while Democrats benefit from a favorable environment, their advantages are not yet anchored in broad confidence in the party itself. 

If Democrats do not improve their economic message, project a more broadly appealing identify, and offer ideas beyond running against Trump, they risk turning a promising political environment into a missed opportunity and Republicans may very well find a way to regain their footing. 

Put another way, the lesson of Virginia is not that the outcome is predetermined; it’s that the fundamentals of this election are still taking shape, and neither party can afford to assume the advantage will hold.

Douglas E. Schoen and Carly Cooperman are pollsters and partners with the public opinion company Schoen Cooperman Research based in New York. They are co-authors of the book “America: Unite or Die.”

data.title: Trump says dinner shooter was 'blur': 'NFL should sign him up'
data.description:
President Trump said that the shooter at the White House Correspondents’ Association dinner was a “blur” and cracked a joke that the NFL “should sign him up.”

“His speed was rather incredible. Actually, it was, it was like a blur,” the president remarked about the gunman in an interview with CBS News’s Norah O’Donnell on “60 Minutes.”

“How did he get that close, with the place swarming with security?” O’Donnell asked.

“I will say, look, I say it because I’m a big fan of the people of law enforcement. And, you know, some of these people, they may be crazy, but they’re not stupid, and they figure things out. He ran 45 yards, they say, and he just went to it, and then, boom, he popped through it,” Trump responded.

“I mean, he ran like — I think the NFL should sign him up — he was fast. When you look at it on tape, it’s almost like a blur,” the president added. 

The president was on Sunday’s “60 Minutes” episode to talk about the shooting Saturday at the annual White House Correspondents’ Association dinner. A heavily armed man ran through security at the Washington Hilton shortly after the dinner started, exchanging gunfire with law enforcement officials prior to being apprehended.

Several media reports have identified the suspected gunman as Cole Allen, from Torrance, Calif.

The suspected shooter was likely targeting Trump and other administration officials, the White House said.

“What was supposed to be a fun night at the @WHCA dinner with President Trump delivering jokes and celebrating free speech was hijacked by a depraved crazy person who sought to assassinate the President and kill as many top Trump administration officials as possible,” White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt said on the social platform X Sunday.

data.title: Trump backing Secret Service leadership after correspondents' dinner shooting
data.description:
President Trump is standing by Secret Service leadership following the shooting at the White House Correspondents’ Dinner on Saturday.

In a statement, a senior White House official said Trump thinks the Secret Service did “an excellent job” of neutralizing the shooter and moving the president, along with first lady Melania Trump and Vice President Vance to safety.

The official also said that White House chief of staff Susie Wiles would hold a meeting at the White House early this week with the White House operations team, Secret Service and Department of Homeland Security leadership to discuss protocol and practice for large events involving Trump.

“The meeting will discuss the processes and procedures that worked to stop Saturday’s attempt, while exploring additional options to ensure all relevant components are doing everything possible to secure the many major events planned for President Trump in the months ahead as he gears up to celebrate America 250,” the official said.

Trump was swiftly removed from the dais at the front of the Washington Hilton ballroom to a special presidential suite, which was created to protect the commander in chief after former President Reagan was shot at the same hotel in 1981.

CBS News reported Sunday, citing both administration and law enforcement officials, that the suspected gunman wrote a manifesto stating he wanted to target White House officials “prioritized from highest-ranking to lowest.” An administration official confirmed to The Hill that the suspect wrote this manifesto.

Questions have risen over how a shooter was able to get so close to the president and the event.

Saturday’s incident follows two other assassination attempts on Trump’s life — one in Butler, Pa., and another in West Palm Peach, Fla. Trump was struck by a bullet in July, 2024 at the Pennsylvania event.

data.title: Senate Republican calls for scrapping filibuster to fund DHS after correspondents' dinner shooting
data.description:
Sen. Ron Johnson (R-Wis.) says Republicans should “nuke” the Senate’s filibuster rule if Democrats continue to block Department of Homeland Security (DHS) funding after the third assassination attempt against President Trump, citing “a moment of national danger.”

Johnson acknowledged in an interview with Maria Bartiromo on Fox News’s “Sunday Morning Futures” that Republicans would want to have the filibuster to “block awful socialist, leftist legislation” if Democrats regain control of power.

But he argued “the Senate’s already broken enough” and that, in light of another assassination attempt against Trump and the “national danger” posed by the ongoing military conflict with Iran, it’s time for Republicans to “rip the Band-Aid off” and eliminate the filibuster.

“The Democrats will do it when they get the majority. At a moment of national danger, if Democrats refuse to fund DHS, I would say this would be the time to nuke the filibuster for good,” Johnson said.

Johnson had previously endorsed scrapping the filibuster to pass the SAVE America Act, Trump’s No. 1 legislative priority, which would require people registering to vote to show documented proof of citizenship and require voters to show identification to prove their identities before voting by mail or in person.

Republicans could eliminate the filibuster by setting a new Senate precedent allowing legislation to advance to a final up-or-down vote by requiring only a simple majority of senators to end debate on a measure.

Doing so is considered so controversial in the upper chamber that the procedure has been dubbed “the nuclear option,” because senators say it would be as drastic as detonating a nuclear weapon in a military conflict.

Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) pledged to preserve the filibuster when he ran for the Senate Republican leader’s job in November of 2024.

Republicans during Trump’s first term in 2017 lowered the threshold for advancing a Supreme Court nominee from 60 votes to a simple majority. That allowed them to end the Democratic filibuster of Neil Gorsuch, who was then swiftly confirmed to the high court.

data.title: America cannot afford to wait for a new Fed chair — confirm Kevin Warsh now
data.description:
Kevin Warsh is exactly the kind of Federal Reserve chairman this moment demands: serious about inflation, serious about restoring credibility, and serious about shrinking a central bank that has grown too large, too intrusive and too erratic.  

The Senate should not treat his nomination as routine or allow it to languish in procedural limbo. It should recognize the opportunity in front of it and confirm him as Federal Reserve chairman now.

The need for urgency begins with the Biden-era economic damage with which Americans are still living. For too long, Democrats dismissed rising prices as temporary while embracing heavy spending, weak fiscal discipline, and a governing philosophy that treated inflation-control as optional. American families did not experience inflation as a technical problem, though — they experienced it as a direct assault on their standard of living. Missteps by the Federal Reserve have kept structural inflation above the Fed’s own 2 percent target for far too long. 

What President Trump understands, and what too many Washington insiders still resist admitting, is that economic recovery is not only about growth figures or monthly headlines. It is also about confidence in the nation’s broader economic system. Confidence affects investment decisions, consumer expectations, capital flows, and the perceived seriousness of the country’s governing institutions.  

The Trump administration is trying to repair the damage left behind by years of Democratic mismanagement. That effort begins with restoring confidence in the Federal Reserve, making Warsh’s nomination especially important. He represents a more disciplined and strategically serious approach to monetary policy at a time when the nation can no longer afford confusion, delay or inflation complacency. 

But Warsh’s Senate confirmation is not merely a domestic issue. It is also a question of America’s global financial standing.  

The U.S. dollar remains the world’s leading reserve currency because foreign governments, central banks, institutions and investors continue to believe that American monetary leadership is more credible than the alternatives. This extraordinary position gives the U.S. immense strategic and financial advantages, but it is not guaranteed forever. It rests in significant part on confidence that the Federal Reserve will protect price stability, preserve institutional credibility, and act with seriousness in times of stress. 

That is why Fed credibility matters so profoundly. If foreign investors begin to doubt the Federal Reserve’s judgment or anti-inflation resolve, confidence in the dollar can gradually weaken. Over time, that could affect capital flows, demand for U.S. Treasuries, borrowing costs, and America’s broader economic leverage.  

A country that depends on the confidence of global investors cannot afford a central bank that appears complacent or structurally undisciplined. The next Fed chair must understand that credibility is not symbolic; it is strategic. Kevin Warsh understands that point better than most. 

Warsh has rightly focused his attention on one of the most neglected features of the inflation problem: the Fed’s balance sheet. In his view, inflation cannot be understood only through the lens of short-term interest rates. It must also be understood through the central bank’s enormous footprint in long-term Treasury and mortgage markets.  

For years, the Fed accumulated massive holdings of long-term securities, distorting price signals, suppressing yields, and encouraging the belief that central-bank intervention could become a permanent feature of economic life. That posture did not simply change market mechanics; it weakened discipline throughout the system. 

According to the Federal Reserve’s release dated April 16, the Fed’s overall balance sheet was about $6.7 trillion, including $4.4 trillion in U.S. Treasury securities. That is an enormous figure. Put simply, the Fed remains far too involved in long-term debt markets. Warsh is right to argue that a central bank of this size distorts markets, weakens discipline, and makes it harder to rebuild trust in monetary policy. 

A smaller Fed balance sheet would mean more honest market pricing, less long-term distortion, and a clearer boundary between monetary policy and fiscal accommodation. It would also send a signal, both domestically and internationally, that the U.S. is prepared to return to a more disciplined model of central banking. In that respect, Warsh is offering more than a personnel change. He is offering a framework for structural institutional correction. 

The Senate does not need to believe that Warsh has a perfect answer to every monetary challenge. It only needs to recognize that he is asking the right questions and moving in the right direction.  

The confirmation process should therefore be understood for what it is: a test of whether Washington is prepared to take monetary credibility seriously again. The Fed chair is one of the most important economic officials in the world. Global investors watch the office closely. Foreign central banks watch it closely. American businesses and families watch it closely. Delay sends a message of hesitation at the precise moment when seriousness and decisiveness are needed. 

If the Senate wants to help restore trust in the dollar, in the Fed, and in the broader long-term economic direction of the country, then it should act with urgency by confirming Kevin Warsh as the next Fed chairman without hesitation or delay. 

Jonathan Galaviz served as a senior official in the first Trump administration, working on economic and foreign relations issues. He previously served as chief economist at Global Market Advisors, and is currently managing director of Galaviz & Co., a boutique consulting firm focused on public policy strategy.

data.title: Iran offers US deal to reopen strait without nuclear deal
data.description:
Iran has offered the U.S. a deal to open the Strait of Hormuz, a key waterway for the oil industry, without any terms regarding its nuclear program, according to The Associated Press.

Iran offered to end its restriction of the strait if the U.S. stops a blockade as well as the current conflict against Tehran, two officials in the region told the news service, with talk on its nuclear program to come later.

The U.S.-Israeli war with Iran has lasted around two months, testing the Trump administration’s relationship with some of its allies and leaving a notable impact on the economy. Iran has reportedly inflicted billions of dollars of damage on U.S. military assets and bases in the Gulf region.

Six people familiar with the damage told NBC News that runways, high-end radar systems, dozens of aircraft, warehouses, command headquarters, aircraft hangars and satellite communications infrastructure had been hit by Iranian forces.

Over the weekend, President Trump said his administration had received a new proposal from Iran following his cancellation of a delegation’s trip to Pakistan for additional peace talks.

“They gave us a paper that should have been better. And interestingly, immediately, when I canceled it, within 10 minutes, we got a new paper that was much better,” Trump told reporters Saturday, Bloomberg reported.

Trump told reporters that the U.S. had “all the cards” in the negotiations.

“We’ll deal by telephone, and they can call us any time they want,” the president said.

data.title: Gunfire has eerie echoes of Reagan's shooting outside the same Washington hotel
data.description:
WASHINGTON (AP) — When President Ronald Reagan left the Washington Hilton Hotel and headed for his waiting limousine on a gray March afternoon, he was exposed for mere seconds. That was all it took for a would-be assassin to take aim and fire.

Reagan was hit in the chest and nearly died. Forty-five years later, another gunman is accused of trying to storm into the same hotel’s ballroom during the White House Correspondents’ Association dinner on Saturday night. The suspect fired at least one shot, authorities said, before being subdued in a chaotic scene that forced the evacuation of President Donald Trump and other top administration officials. The gunman never entered the ballroom or was close to the president.

That Hilton has hosted hundreds of large events attended by presidents and other dignitaries since it opened in the 1960s. While on the surface there appear to be similarities in the incidents beyond its location, there are stark differences that highlight how much has changed in the decades since Reagan was shot.

“Security is a lot more robust today than it was then,” said Stephen T. Colo, a former assistant director of the Secret Service. “But you still deal with the same tension involving politicians and the public’s access to them.”

Washington Hilton was built to accommodate presidents

The Washington Hilton Hotel and its cavernous ballroom were designed to be a prime venue for presidential speeches and events. To entice high-profile speakers, primarily the president, architects designed a VIP entrance on the side of the hotel and, one floor below it, a holding room known as the bunker.

In the decade before Reagan was shot, presidents visited the hotel more than a hundred times.

The 1981 shooting was set in motion when Hinckley got on a bus in Los Angeles, where he had been trying to write and sell music, and headed to Washington. There, he planned to hop on another bus to New Haven, Connecticut, to stage a suicide in front of the object of his obsession, movie star Jodie Foster.

In the nation’s capital, he learned Reagan would be speaking at the Washington Hilton on the afternoon of March 30, and he changed his plans. He would try to kill the president to impress the actress.

Hinckley got very close to the president

Outside the hotel that afternoon, Hinkley found himself 15 feet from Reagan as the president headed to his limousine. In a small crowd of onlookers and journalists behind a rope line, the would-be assassin pulled out a gun and fired six shots in 1.7 seconds, wounding Reagan, White House press secretary Jim Brady, District of Columbia Police Officer Thomas Delahanty and Secret Service agent Tim McCarthy.

Reagan was struck below his left armpit, the bullet lodged an inch from his heart. Reagan survived thanks to the quick thinking of Secret Service agent Jerry Parr and the medical personnel at George Washington University Hospital. Hinckley was found not guilty by reason of insanity.

In the wake of the shooting, the Secret Service enhanced security in dozens of ways. The most visible action came when the Secret Service began deploying checkpoints and metal detectors to screen visitors at the White House and at public events. Hinckley did not have to pass through either a checkpoint or metal detector to get so close to the president.

The hotel built a bunker-like garage for the armored limousine to park and drop off and pick up the president at the VIP entrance. The Secret Service and local police assigned more agents and officers to guard presidential events at the Hilton.

Even with such enhancements, former agents said, securing the Hilton is challenging and highlights the tension between protecting politicians and ensuring the public has access to them. The hotel also has many public areas, and it would be hard to shut them down for an event, even one as high profile as the correspondents’ dinner.

That was why the main security checkpoint, they said, was near the ballroom and not in the hotel lobby or entrance — measures that would be disruptive to hundreds of guests and hotel operations. Inside the ballroom, more agents and heavily armed tactical officers were stationed close to the president.

Gunman was stopped at security checkpoint

On Saturday, the suspect sprinted through the checkpoint leading to the ballroom, according to video posted by Trump. The video shows officers and agents pivoting and pointing guns at the man as he ran away. The assailant was quickly subdued and was not injured, officials said. An officer was shot in a bullet-resistant vest, officials said, but was not seriously hurt.

Acting U.S. Attorney General Todd Blanche said Sunday that the gunman was likely seeking to target the president and members of the administration.

The assailant is suspected of having traveled by train from California to Chicago and then on to Washington, where in recent days he checked in as a guest at the hotel, Blanche said.

Law enforcement officials familiar with the matter identified, to The Associated Press, the suspect as 31-year-old Cole Tomas Allen of Torrance, California. Cole sent writings to family members minutes before the shooting referring to himself as a “Friendly Federal Assassin,” railing against Trump administration policies and signaling what investigators increasingly believe was a politically driven attack, according to another law enforcement official who, like the others, was not to authorized discuss the investigation publicly and spoke on condition of anonymity.

The writings made repeated references to Trump, the official said, without directly naming the president and alluded to grievances over a range of administration actions

data.title: Making vaccination optional threatens military readiness
data.description:
Military readiness is no accident. I saw this firsthand as a combat-decorated U.S. Army special forces veteran, and later as the 17th U.S. surgeon general. Readiness is built on discipline, leadership, and adherence to evidence-based practices that protect the force and ensure mission success.

Today, unfortunately, the Pentagon is adopting proposals to make prevention — particularly vaccination — optional. This represents a fundamental misunderstanding of military readiness and force protection.

In combat, we learned quickly that preventable threats degrade a unit just as effectively if they come from disease as if they come from enemy fire. The mission demands that we mitigate risk wherever possible using every available evidence-based tool.

Recently, public health policy has been challenged in ways that distort the reality of what prevention achieves. That distorted reality should generate better communication, better education, and better leadership — not abandonment of proven, evidence-based measures. If confidence has been weakened, the answer is not to retreat from science, but to commit ourselves even more to explain it clearly and apply it responsibly.

This principle is not new — it is embedded in the very origins of our military.

George Washington understood that disease could devastate a fighting force more effectively than any opposing army could. During the Revolutionary War, with smallpox threatening the Continental Army, he ordered the mass inoculation of his troops in 1777. It was an extraordinary and controversial decision, but it was firmly grounded in science, risk mitigation, and mission readiness.

Historians widely credit Washington’s action with preserving the force that ultimately secured American independence. Washington, ahead of his time, established an enduring American military tradition: health protection for our forces through science-based prevention.

This protection is not optional, any more than weapons maintenance, physical training, or intelligence are optional. It is essential to readiness and national security, to mission success and to the survival of the force. To suggest otherwise is to accept avoidable risk and to compromise readiness.

Infectious disease does not respect ideology, rank, or mission timetables. It exploits gaps in preparedness. It spreads where discipline falters. And it can degrade readiness without firing a single shot.

Military leaders are entrusted with the lives of those they lead. That trust demands decisions grounded in science, experience, and a clear understanding of risk. Making prevention optional undermines that trust and places service members in harm’s way unnecessarily.

As someone who has stood the watch in combat, in trauma rooms and in national public health leadership, I have learned a simple but enduring lesson: The threats we can prevent are the ones we must take most seriously. Failure to do so is not an act of freedom, but an abdication of leadership.

Our service members deserve policies that reflect the full weight of science, the lessons of history, and the responsibility we bear before sending troops into harm’s way. The best military in the world requires a disciplined approach to prevention.

Therefore, we must stand the watch for prevention — even when it is inconvenient — because readiness delayed is readiness denied.

Dr. Richard Carmona, a decorated combat veteran of the U.S. Army special forces, served as the 17th U.S. surgeon general.

data.title: Woman gives birth on Delta flight before emergency landing in Oregon
data.description:
PORTLAND, Ore. (KOIN) – It’s a bird! It’s a plane! … with a baby?

A woman went into labor during a roughly 5-hour flight from Atlanta to Portland, Oregon, on Friday.

A doctor and two nurses on board assisted flight attendants to successfully deliver the baby roughly 30 minutes before Delta Flight 478 came in for an emergency landing in Portland, a Delta spokesperson told Nexstar’s KOIN.

Emergency medical services responded to the plane when it landed at around 10 p.m.

“We extend our sincere thanks to the crew and medical volunteers on board who stepped in to provide care to a customer onboard prior to landing in Portland,” Delta’s spokesperson said. “The health and safety of our customers is always our top priority, and we wish the new family all the best.”

According to the Port of Portland, the pair were in “stable condition” when EMS arrived.

This is at least the second birth in the skies this month.

A pregnant passenger went into labor aboard a Caribbean Airlines flight out of Jamaica, bound for New York’s John F. Kennedy International Airport, on April 4. Unlike the Portland flight, no emergency declaration was made, though the mother gave birth before the plane landed.

Nexstar’s Addy Bink contributed to this report.

data.title: A child abduction case shows how the media hide the ball on 'gender identity'
data.description:
Journalism is about sharing plain facts as clearly and as widely as possible. Its great enemy is euphemism. No reader wants to waste time trying to decode deliberately deceptive language or reading between the lines of news reports. If you’re looking for a puzzle, you would probably rather play the Sudoku.

Unfortunately, everyone is being forced to do a lot of news decoding nowadays.

Just look at the New York Times and Associated Press’s coverage of a recent child abduction case. The FBI arrested the father of a 10-year-old boy on charges of international parental kidnapping. But because the accused identifies as transgender, journalists are obscuring the basic details with activist-speak, starting with the first paragraph.

For a story involving such a serious allegation as this one, the Times headline quite nearly framed the FBI as the bad guys: “U.S. Sends Plane to Cuba to Get Child in Transgender Custody Case.” The AP chose: “Trump administration flies 10-year-old back from Cuba amid custody fight involving gender identity.”

Neither headline is false in the strictest sense, yet both are masterpieces of evasion.

The story is not so anodyne as a mere custody dispute, but nor is it about the Trump administration meddling in “gender identity” issues. It is about a divorced father allegedly kidnapping and smuggling his 10-year-old son out of the country in flagrant violation of custody laws.

Unfortunately, any reader trying to understand the basic facts of this case must first hack through a thicket of “she-her” pronouns in reference to the father and various “they-them” circumlocutions.

Here are the facts: A biological male once known as Eri Ethington fathered a son with his then-wife. Shortly after the birth of his child, Eri began living under the name “Rose” and identifying as transgender.

A few years later, the transgender father began to push hard for his son to get transition surgery, according to Rose’s brother. The boy’s mother opposed this and a bitter divorce ensued, producing a shared-custody agreement.

This year, Rose and his new partner, Blue Inessa-Ethington (formerly Carly Ann Crosby), told the mother that they were taking the boy on a camping trip to Canada, according to an FBI affidavit.

A hotel was picked out, and a campsite selected. The trip was scheduled to wrap up by April 3, at which point the son was supposed to return to his mother. April 3 came and went, and the mother hadn’t heard anything, even though her son had phoned on March 28 to tell her that they had arrived in Canada.

The mother tried to contact her ex-husband, but both Rose’s and Blue’s phones were turned off. All calls went directly to voicemail, according to the affidavit. The mother then contacted police.

Instead of the proposed camping trip, Rose and company had fled from Canada to Havana.

Family members told investigators they believed the son might be in danger, citing preexisting concerns that Rose had manipulated the child into denying his biological sex. One family member also expressed concern that Rose might have fled to Cuba to subject the child to gender-reassignment surgery — something Rose had been advocating for at least since the child was five years old.

An FBI search of the couple’s home uncovered handwritten to-do lists, including “learn Spanish” and “empty USU bank,” and notes that read, “10k withdrawal — cash to have,” and “10k emergency.”

Blue withdrew $10,000 from her checking account on March 26.

Investigators also recovered notes with instructions from a therapist in Washington, D.C., directing the transgender father to send the therapist $10,000 and providing guidance on “gender affirming medical care for children.”

Investigators soon determined that Rose had recently quit his job; Blue had stopped communicating with her employer. Based on the “extensive planning and preparation” documented by law enforcement officials, wrote the FBI affiant, Rose and Blue were likely not planning to return to the U.S. at all.

On April 13, a Utah court granted the mother sole custody and ordered the boy returned immediately.

Cuban authorities found Rose’s group shortly thereafter and turned them over to U.S. officials, who had arrived in person for the handoff. Rose and Blue were deported aboard a Boeing 757, arrested upon landing in Richmond, Va., and arraigned the same day.

The boy is safely back with his mother. The criminal case is now pending in federal court in Utah.

Even a Muppet could follow this plot. Yet despite what seems a straightforward criminal allegation, the Times and the AP frame the story as a gender-identity case, in which the Trump administration is cruelly blocking gender-affirming care for a young child.

Particularly strange is the media fixation on the gender-reassignment angle, which is based on a single line in the affidavit. This modest detail, which the Justice Department presented as merely a theory posited by a worried family member, gets outsized media play as the central theme of the story.

The case “appeared to touch on a major policy issue for the Trump administration: cracking down and restricting transition surgeries for minors, procedures that the administration has characterized as ‘surgical and chemical mutilations,'” reports the Times.

Meanwhile, the AP dedicated the final four paragraphs of its story to detailing the Trump administration’s efforts to “cut off … gender-affirming care for minors,” while assuring readers that “gender-affirming surgery” for children in the U.S. is “rare” and that Cuba would never consent to it under such circumstances.

But of course, the defendants in this case likely did not flee to Cuba because it’s especially pro-transgender or because it offers state-funded reassignment surgery. They probably chose Cuba for its lack of extradition agreements with the U.S. You can’t enforce U.S. custody law in a country that doesn’t recognize U.S. law at all — or at least, that’s how the thinking goes. It didn’t quite work out that way in practice.

It would be nice if some of these news outlets expressed half as much alarm over the alleged international kidnapping scheme as they do over the Justice Department’s effort to bring a child, so badly treated, home to his mother.

T. Becket Adams is a journalist and critic living in Washington.
